title: Carbohydrates and lipids
summary: Carbohydrates and lipids are where the theme of form and function comes alive at the molecular level. This lesson builds the whole of B1.1: monosaccharides and the α/β forms of glucose, disaccharides, and the three key polysaccharides (starch, glycogen, cellulose) — and above all how each structure is matched to its job, storage versus structural. It works through condensation and hydrolysis and the glycosidic and ester bonds they make and break, then turns to lipids: triglycerides, phospholipids, saturated versus unsaturated fatty acids, the functions of lipids, and a careful comparison of lipids and carbohydrates as energy stores. The model answer is laid out exactly the way our marking engine awards Paper 2 marks, so you can see how each distinct point earns its credit.
learningObjectives: Describe the structure of monosaccharides, including the α and β ring forms of glucose, and how they join into disaccharides and polysaccharides.
Explain how the structures of starch, glycogen and cellulose suit their storage or structural functions.
Explain how condensation forms glycosidic and ester bonds while hydrolysis breaks them, adding or removing water.
Describe triglycerides, phospholipids and the difference between saturated and unsaturated fatty acids, relating each to function.
Compare and contrast lipids and carbohydrates as energy-storage molecules, and present the comparison the way our marking engine awards analytic marks.
levelNote: Common to SL and HL at this level.

simpleExplanation.title: Sugars, Chains and Fats
simpleExplanation.summary: Carbohydrates and lipids are both built from carbon, hydrogen and oxygen, but they are put together differently and do different jobs. Carbohydrates run from single sugars up to long chains for quick energy, storage or structure; lipids are oily, water-hating molecules used mainly for long-term energy storage, insulation and building membranes.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Picture a warehouse. Loose coins by the door (single sugars, glucose) are spent instantly. Rolls of coins stacked on shelves (starch and glycogen) are the everyday savings — easy to break open when cash is needed. The steel shelving itself (cellulose) is made of the same coins locked in a rigid frame so they can never be spent; it is there for strength, not spending. And in the basement vault sit dense gold bars (lipids): far more value packed into far less space and weight, perfect when you need to store a lot of energy for a long time without carrying bulk.
steps: Identify the monomer: a single sugar ring (monosaccharide) for a carbohydrate, or glycerol plus fatty acids for a triglyceride.
Classify the molecule: single unit (monosaccharide), a pair (disaccharide) or a long chain (polysaccharide) for carbohydrates; a lipid is glycerol joined to fatty acids.
Track the reaction: condensation joins subunits and removes water, forming a glycosidic bond (sugars) or an ester bond (lipids); hydrolysis adds water to break those bonds.
Match structure to function: branched glycogen releases glucose fast, straight β-glucose chains in cellulose give strength, and long hydrocarbon tails make lipids an energy-dense, waterproof store.

flashcards.back: The simplest carbohydrate — a single sugar unit and the monomer for larger carbohydrates. Examples: glucose ($C_6H_{12}O_6$), fructose and ribose. Soluble, sweet and readily respired for energy.

Two ring forms of glucose differing only in the position of the hydroxyl (–OH) group on carbon 1: DOWN in α-glucose, UP in β-glucose. α-glucose builds starch and glycogen; β-glucose builds cellulose. The tiny difference is why one is a store and the other is structural.

Two subunits join to form a larger molecule and a water molecule is REMOVED. Builds glycosidic bonds (between sugars) and ester bonds (between glycerol and fatty acids). One bond formed = one water released.
A larger molecule is split into subunits by ADDING water — the reverse of condensation. One water molecule is used per bond broken. This is the basis of digestion.
The covalent bond joining two monosaccharides, formed by condensation. A 1,4-glycosidic bond joins carbon 1 to carbon 4; 1,6 bonds create the branch points in glycogen and amylopectin.

The glucose store of plants, made of α-glucose. Amylose is unbranched and coils into a compact helix; amylopectin is branched. Insoluble, so it does not affect water potential and does not diffuse out of the cell.

The glucose store of animals (liver and muscle), made of α-glucose. MORE highly branched than starch, giving many free ends where enzymes can act, so glucose is released quickly to meet high metabolic demand.

A structural polysaccharide of plant cell walls, made of β-glucose in straight, unbranched chains. Alternate units flip 180°, so chains lie parallel and hydrogen-bond into strong microfibrils that resist turgor pressure.
A lipid formed by condensation of one glycerol with THREE fatty acids, joined by three ester bonds (three water molecules released). The main long-term energy store in animals.

A glycerol joined to TWO fatty acids and one phosphate group. The phosphate head is hydrophilic and the fatty-acid tails are hydrophobic, so phospholipids form the bilayer that is the basis of all cell membranes.

Saturated fatty acids have NO C=C double bonds (chain saturated with hydrogen), pack closely and are usually solid at room temperature. Unsaturated fatty acids have one or more C=C double bonds; a cis double bond kinks the chain, prevents close packing and makes them liquid (oils).
The covalent bond joining the carboxyl group of a fatty acid to a hydroxyl group of glycerol, formed by condensation with the loss of water. Three ester bonds hold a triglyceride together.

flashcards.front: Why lipids store more energy per gram
flashcards.back: Lipid hydrocarbon tails are highly reduced (rich in C–H bonds, little oxygen), so oxidising them releases about 38 kJ g⁻¹ versus about 17 kJ g⁻¹ for carbohydrate. Lipids are also hydrophobic and stored anhydrous, so they add no extra water mass — energy-dense and compact.
Condensation RELEASES water and BUILDS a bond; hydrolysis USES water and BREAKS a bond. Saying 'water is added to form a glycosidic bond' reverses the process and loses the mark.

quickCheck.prompt: Two glucose molecules join to form maltose. What is correct about this reaction?
options: A water molecule is added and an ester bond forms
A water molecule is released and a glycosidic bond forms
A water molecule is released and an ester bond forms
A water molecule is added and a glycosidic bond forms
quickCheck.answer: A water molecule is released and a glycosidic bond forms

quickCheck.prompt: Glycogen releases glucose faster than starch mainly because glycogen is:
options: Made of β-glucose rather than α-glucose
More highly branched, giving more free ends for enzymes to act on
Soluble, so it dissolves and diffuses to where it is needed
Held together by ester bonds that break more easily
quickCheck.answer: More highly branched, giving more free ends for enzymes to act on

quickCheck.prompt: Cellulose is strong and structural, whereas starch is a store. The key structural reason is that cellulose is built from:
options: β-glucose, so straight chains hydrogen-bond into microfibrils
α-glucose, so it coils into a compact helix
Fructose units linked by ester bonds
Branched α-glucose with many 1,6 bonds
quickCheck.answer: β-glucose, so straight chains hydrogen-bond into microfibrils

quickCheck.prompt: An oil is liquid at room temperature while a fat is solid. This is best explained by the oil's fatty acids being:
options: Saturated, so straight chains pack closely together
Unsaturated, so cis double bonds kink the chains and prevent close packing
Shorter, so they contain less stored energy
Joined to glycerol by glycosidic rather than ester bonds
quickCheck.answer: Unsaturated, so cis double bonds kink the chains and prevent close packing

quickCheck.prompt: Per gram, lipids store roughly twice the energy of carbohydrates. The main reason is that lipid molecules are:
options: Larger, so a single molecule weighs more
More highly reduced (more C–H bonds, little oxygen), releasing more energy on oxidation
Soluble, so they are respired more completely
Made of β-glucose, which has more energy than α-glucose
quickCheck.answer: More highly reduced (more C–H bonds, little oxygen), releasing more energy on oxidation

faq.q: What is the actual difference between α-glucose and β-glucose, and why does it matter so much?
faq.a: Both have the formula $C_6H_{12}O_6$ and the same ring; they differ only in the position of the hydroxyl group on carbon 1 — pointing down in α-glucose and up in β-glucose. That looks trivial, but it changes how the units line up when they polymerise. Chains of α-glucose curl and branch into compact stores (starch, glycogen); chains of β-glucose can only join if alternate units flip 180°, which produces straight, tightly hydrogen-bonded fibres (cellulose). One molecule, two orientations, and the whole storage-versus-structural distinction follows.

faq.q: Why can't humans digest cellulose when we digest starch easily?
faq.a: Our enzymes (amylase, maltase) hydrolyse the α-1,4 glycosidic bonds in starch, but they do not fit the β-1,4 bonds in cellulose, and humans make no cellulase. So cellulose passes through as dietary fibre. Herbivores such as cows rely on symbiotic gut microbes that do produce cellulase, which is why they can extract energy from grass and we cannot.

faq.q: Why is a phospholipid a bilayer former when a triglyceride is not?
faq.a: A triglyceride has three fatty-acid tails and no charged group, so the whole molecule is hydrophobic and simply clumps away from water. A phospholipid replaces one fatty acid with a phosphate group, giving it a hydrophilic head and two hydrophobic tails. In water this dual nature drives the molecules to line up with heads facing the water and tails facing inward — a bilayer — which is the structural basis of every cell membrane.

faq.q: If lipids store so much more energy per gram, why do we bother storing carbohydrate at all?
faq.a: Because the two stores answer different needs. Carbohydrate (glycogen) is quick to mobilise, water-soluble to handle and can be respired without oxygen for short bursts, so it is the go-to for immediate demand. Lipid is energy-dense and light, ideal for long-term reserves, insulation and buoyancy, but it is slower to mobilise and needs oxygen. Organisms keep a small, fast carbohydrate store and a large, compact lipid store — the right tool for each timescale.

sections.content: Carbohydrates and lipids are the two great families of energy molecules, and B1.1 is really a lesson in the theme of form and function: in almost every case a molecule's shape explains its job. A single change in the orientation of one hydroxyl group turns a storage sugar into a structural fibre; a single kink from a double bond turns a solid fat into a liquid oil; a single swapped tail turns a fat store into a membrane builder. This lesson works through monosaccharides, disaccharides and polysaccharides, the condensation and hydrolysis reactions that build and break them, and then the lipids — triglycerides, phospholipids and the saturated/unsaturated distinction — before comparing carbohydrates and lipids as energy stores in the way examiners actually mark it.
Monosaccharides: the single sugars

Carbohydrates are made of carbon, hydrogen and oxygen, with hydrogen and oxygen roughly in the 2:1 ratio of water, giving the general formula $C_n(H_2O)_n$. The monomer is the monosaccharide, or single sugar. Glucose ($C_6H_{12}O_6$) is the central example: a six-carbon sugar, soluble in water, sweet, and the primary respiratory substrate of nearly every cell. Other monosaccharides you should know are fructose (fruit sugar) and ribose (a five-carbon sugar, $C_5H_{10}O_5$, that forms part of RNA). In solution glucose closes into a ring, and that ring can form in two ways.

The two ring forms of glucose differ only in where the hydroxyl (–OH) group on carbon 1 sits. In **α-glucose** it points below the ring; in **β-glucose** it points above. This single difference has enormous consequences: α-glucose polymers become compact energy stores (starch and glycogen), while β-glucose polymers become strong structural fibres (cellulose). It is the clearest example in the topic of a tiny change in form producing a completely different function.

items: **Monosaccharide** = single sugar monomer; glucose ($C_6H_{12}O_6$), fructose and ribose are the key examples.
Monosaccharides are soluble, sweet and readily respired to release energy.
**α-glucose:** –OH on carbon 1 points DOWN — builds starch and glycogen.
**β-glucose:** –OH on carbon 1 points UP — builds cellulose.
Same formula, different orientation: the root of the storage-versus-structural split.

sections.content: Condensation and hydrolysis: building and breaking

Monosaccharides are linked into larger carbohydrates by **condensation reactions**. In a condensation reaction two subunits join and a molecule of water is removed; the new covalent link between two sugars is a **glycosidic bond**. Two monosaccharides give a disaccharide — for example glucose + glucose → maltose, glucose + fructose → sucrose, and glucose + galactose → lactose. Add many monosaccharides in this way and you build a polysaccharide. Crucially, every bond formed releases exactly one water molecule, so joining $n$ sugars into a chain releases $n-1$ waters.

The reverse process is **hydrolysis** (hydro = water, lysis = splitting). Here a water molecule is added across a bond to break it, regenerating the smaller subunits. Hydrolysis is how digestion works: amylase hydrolyses starch to maltose, and maltase hydrolyses maltose to glucose, each step using water to cut a glycosidic bond. The same condensation/hydrolysis logic applies to every biological polymer, including the ester bonds in lipids, so it is worth learning the pattern once and applying it everywhere.

items: **Condensation:** subunits join, a glycosidic (or ester) bond forms, one water molecule is RELEASED per bond.
**Hydrolysis:** water is ADDED to break a bond and release subunits — the exact reverse of condensation.
Disaccharides: maltose (glucose+glucose), sucrose (glucose+fructose), lactose (glucose+galactose).
Digestion is hydrolysis; polymer synthesis in the cell is condensation.
The same rule governs the ester bonds of triglycerides — one water per bond.

sections.content: Polysaccharides: structure suited to function

Three polysaccharides show, better than anything else in the course, how structure is matched to function. **Starch** is the glucose store of plants. It is a mixture of amylose, an unbranched chain of α-glucose joined by 1,4-glycosidic bonds that coils into a compact helix, and amylopectin, which adds 1,6 branch points. Being a large, insoluble molecule, starch does not dissolve to alter the cell's water potential and cannot diffuse out of the cell — ideal properties for a store you want to stay put and stay inert until needed.

**Glycogen** is the equivalent store in animals, held in liver and muscle. Like starch it is a polymer of α-glucose, but it is **more highly branched**. Each branch ends in a free end where enzymes can attach, so a highly branched molecule can be hydrolysed at many points at once, releasing glucose rapidly. Animals, with their high and variable metabolic rate, need exactly that fast release — so the extra branching is function-driven. **Cellulose** is different in kind: a structural polysaccharide of plant cell walls built from β-glucose. Because β-glucose has its carbon-1 –OH pointing up, alternate units must rotate 180° to bond, producing long, straight, unbranched chains. Parallel chains hydrogen-bond to one another to form microfibrils of great tensile strength, which resist the turgor pressure pushing outward on the cell wall. Straight and strong for support; branched and compact for storage — same glucose building block, opposite jobs.

items: **Starch (plant store):** α-glucose; amylose (helical, unbranched) + amylopectin (branched); insoluble, so osmotically inactive and retained in the cell.
**Glycogen (animal store):** α-glucose, MORE branched than starch → many free ends → rapid glucose release for high metabolic demand.
**Cellulose (plant structure):** β-glucose; straight chains hydrogen-bond into microfibrils → high tensile strength for the cell wall.
Storage polysaccharides are compact and easily broken down; structural ones are strong and resistant to breakdown.
The α/β choice of monomer is what makes a store or a fibre.

sections.question: Explain how the structure of glycogen makes it well suited to its function as an energy store in animals. [3]
sections.solution:
**Model answer.**
Glycogen is a polymer of α-glucose, so it is a large, insoluble molecule that stores many glucose units compactly without dissolving or affecting the cell's water potential. It is highly branched, which gives many free ends where enzymes can act, so glucose can be hydrolysed and released rapidly to meet the high metabolic demand of active animals. Being insoluble, it stays inside the cell rather than diffusing away.

---
**How our marking engine awards the 3 marks** — each distinct valid point scores 1 (maximum 3):

- **Point 1:** it is a polymer of (α-)glucose / a compact, insoluble store, so it is osmotically inactive / does not affect water potential.
- **Point 2:** it is highly branched, giving many free ends / terminal glucose units for enzymes to act on.
- **Point 3:** the branching allows rapid hydrolysis and glucose release to meet (high) metabolic demand.

*The engine credits distinct ideas, so 'it is branched' and 'branching gives fast release' only score twice if the consequence (fast release) is actually stated — otherwise the two count as one point.*

sections.content: Lipids: triglycerides and phospholipids

Lipids are a chemically varied group united by one property: they are hydrophobic and do not dissolve in water. The most important for energy storage is the **triglyceride**. A triglyceride forms when one molecule of glycerol condenses with **three fatty acids**. Each fatty acid's carboxyl (–COOH) group reacts with one of glycerol's three hydroxyl groups, forming an **ester bond** and releasing one water molecule — so making a triglyceride releases three waters in total. A fatty acid is a long hydrocarbon chain ending in a carboxyl group; the long, energy-rich, water-repelling tails are what make triglycerides such a dense, waterproof store.

A **phospholipid** is a close relative with a decisive twist: one of the three fatty acids is replaced by a **phosphate group**. This gives the molecule two personalities — a hydrophilic (water-loving) phosphate head and two hydrophobic (water-hating) fatty-acid tails. When surrounded by water, phospholipids spontaneously arrange into a **bilayer**, heads outward toward the water and tails inward away from it. This bilayer is the fundamental structure of every cell membrane, so the same lipid chemistry that stores energy in one form builds the boundary of the cell in another.

items: **Triglyceride:** 1 glycerol + 3 fatty acids, joined by 3 ester bonds (condensation, 3 waters released). Main long-term energy store.
**Fatty acid:** long hydrocarbon tail + carboxyl group; the tail carries the stored energy and repels water.
**Phospholipid:** 1 glycerol + 2 fatty acids + 1 phosphate group.
Phospholipids are amphipathic — hydrophilic head, hydrophobic tails — so they form the membrane bilayer.
Same ester-bond chemistry underlies energy storage and membrane structure.

sections.content: Saturated and unsaturated fatty acids

Fatty-acid tails vary in one important way: whether their carbon chain contains any carbon–carbon double bonds. A **saturated** fatty acid has no C=C double bonds — every carbon is 'saturated' with the maximum number of hydrogen atoms. Its straight chains pack closely together, so saturated fats (like butter and animal fat) are usually solid at room temperature. An **unsaturated** fatty acid has one or more C=C double bonds; monounsaturated has one, polyunsaturated has several. A cis double bond puts a permanent kink in the chain, so unsaturated fatty acids cannot pack tightly, and unsaturated fats (like olive oil and other plant oils) are usually liquid at room temperature. The degree of saturation therefore controls whether a triglyceride is a solid 'fat' or a liquid 'oil' — a direct structure-to-property link — and it also matters biologically, because unsaturated tails keep membranes fluid.

items: **Saturated:** no C=C double bonds; straight chains pack closely; solid at room temperature (fats).
**Unsaturated:** one (mono-) or more (poly-) C=C double bonds; a cis double bond kinks the chain; liquid at room temperature (oils).
Kinks prevent close packing, lowering the melting point — pure structure explaining a physical property.
Unsaturated tails also keep cell membranes fluid at lower temperatures.

sections.content: Examiners often ask you to link a fatty acid's structure to whether it is a fat or an oil. Do not just say 'unsaturated fats are liquid'; say WHY: the C=C double bond (cis) causes a kink, the kink prevents close packing, weaker intermolecular attraction lowers the melting point, so it is liquid at room temperature. The mark scheme rewards the chain of reasoning, not the bare fact.
Functions of lipids and comparison with carbohydrates

Lipids do far more than store energy. Their major functions are **long-term energy storage** (triglycerides packed into adipose tissue), **thermal insulation** (a subcutaneous fat layer slows heat loss, vital in mammals and especially in the blubber of marine mammals), physical **protection** of organs, buoyancy, and — through phospholipids — forming the **membranes** of every cell. Some lipids, such as steroid hormones, even act as chemical signals. As an energy store specifically, lipids and carbohydrates play complementary roles, and understanding the trade-off between them is a favourite exam theme.

The headline difference is energy density. Lipids release about **38 kJ per gram** when respired, roughly double the **17 kJ per gram** from carbohydrate. This is because fatty-acid tails are highly reduced — they are rich in energy-releasing C–H bonds and contain little oxygen — so their oxidation liberates more energy. Lipids are also hydrophobic and stored anhydrous (with no associated water), whereas glycogen is stored hydrated, adding mass. Together these make lipids a lighter, more compact reserve — an advantage for animals that must carry their store around, and the reason migrating birds and hibernating mammals rely on fat. Carbohydrate, however, is not obsolete: glycogen is more rapidly mobilised, is water-soluble and easy to transport, and can be respired anaerobically for short bursts. So organisms keep a small, fast carbohydrate store for immediate demand and a large, energy-dense lipid store for the long term.

items: **Functions of lipids:** long-term energy storage, thermal insulation, protection of organs, buoyancy, and membrane formation (phospholipids).
**Energy density:** lipid ≈ 38 kJ g⁻¹ vs carbohydrate ≈ 17 kJ g⁻¹ — lipids store about twice as much per gram.
**Why:** lipid tails are highly reduced (many C–H bonds, little oxygen), releasing more energy on oxidation.
**Mass advantage:** lipids are hydrophobic and stored anhydrous; glycogen is stored hydrated, adding water mass.
**Carbohydrate advantage:** faster to mobilise, water-soluble/easily transported, can be respired anaerobically.
Result: small fast carbohydrate store + large dense lipid store, each suited to its timescale.

sections.question: An athlete is comparing fuel sources. Calculate and compare the total energy available from 60 g of pure carbohydrate and 60 g of pure lipid, using the standard energy values (carbohydrate ≈ 17 kJ g⁻¹, lipid ≈ 38 kJ g⁻¹). [3]
sections.solution:
**Step 1 — recall the energy values.**
Carbohydrate ≈ 17 kJ g⁻¹; lipid ≈ 38 kJ g⁻¹.  [1 mark for both values]

**Step 2 — carbohydrate energy.**
$60 \text{ g} \times 17 \text{ kJ g}^{-1} = 1020 \text{ kJ}$.  [1 mark]

**Step 3 — lipid energy and comparison.**
$60 \text{ g} \times 38 \text{ kJ g}^{-1} = 2280 \text{ kJ}$.
The lipid provides $2280 - 1020 = 1260 \text{ kJ}$ more — over twice the energy for the same mass.  [1 mark for calculation and comparison]

*If a wrong energy value were used in Step 1, the method marks in Steps 2–3 are still awarded by error-carried-forward, provided the working is shown.*

sections.content: Common mistakes examiners penalise

items: **Reversing condensation and hydrolysis** — condensation RELEASES water and forms a bond; hydrolysis ADDS water and breaks one. 'Water is added to make a glycosidic bond' loses the mark.
**Confusing glycosidic and ester bonds** — glycosidic bonds join sugars; ester bonds join glycerol to fatty acids. Do not write 'ester bond between two glucose molecules'.
**Mixing up α- and β-glucose** — starch and glycogen are α-glucose; cellulose is β-glucose. Getting these the wrong way round breaks the whole structure–function argument.
**Saying glycogen releases glucose fast because it is soluble** — it is the BRANCHING (many free ends for enzymes) that gives fast release, not solubility. The store is actually large and insoluble.
**Explaining oils are liquid with just 'they are unsaturated'** — you must state the cause: cis C=C double bond → kink → chains cannot pack closely → lower melting point.

**Claiming lipids store more energy because 'the molecules are bigger'** — the reason is that lipid tails are highly reduced (more C–H bonds, little oxygen), so oxidation releases more energy per gram.

**Giving two separate lists instead of comparing** — in a 'compare and contrast' question, the marks are for comparative statements linking carbohydrate AND lipid in each point, not a paragraph on each in isolation.
**Wrong count of ester bonds/water** — a triglyceride has THREE ester bonds and releases THREE water molecules; a phospholipid has two fatty acids and a phosphate.

sections.content: Model answer — marked the way our engine marks it

'Compare and contrast' questions are marked analytically: each distinct valid comparison point is worth one mark. The engine is looking for COMPARATIVE statements — sentences that mention both carbohydrate and lipid and say how they are alike or how they differ — not two separate lists. A correct point earns the answer mark (A); error-carried-forward means an earlier slip does not sink later independent points; and equivalents are accepted as long as the comparison is genuine. Study how each mark below is tied to a specific comparison rather than to loose phrasing.

sections.question: Compare and contrast the use of carbohydrates and lipids as energy-storage molecules in organisms. [4]
sections.solution:
**Model answer.**
Both carbohydrates and lipids are used by organisms as energy-storage molecules and both are built and broken down by condensation and hydrolysis. However, lipids release about twice as much energy per gram as carbohydrates (≈ 38 kJ g⁻¹ versus ≈ 17 kJ g⁻¹) because lipid tails are more highly reduced. Lipids are stored anhydrously and are hydrophobic, so they add no extra water mass, whereas carbohydrate (glycogen) is stored hydrated and is more compact only relative to its solubility. Carbohydrate stores are more rapidly mobilised and are water-soluble, so they are better for short-term or immediate energy demand, while lipids are better suited to long-term storage. Both are insoluble in their stored polymer/triglyceride form, so neither affects the cell's water potential.

---
**How our marking engine awards the 4 marks** — each distinct valid COMPARISON point scores 1 (maximum 4):

- **Point 1 (similarity):** both are used as energy stores / both are respired to release energy / both built by condensation and broken by hydrolysis.
- **Point 2 (difference, A):** lipids store more energy per gram than carbohydrates (≈ 38 vs ≈ 17 kJ g⁻¹) — accept 'about twice as much'.
- **Point 3 (difference):** lipids are hydrophobic/stored anhydrous so add no water mass, whereas carbohydrate/glycogen is stored hydrated — accept the equivalent 'lipids are more compact / lighter store'.
- **Point 4 (difference):** carbohydrate is more rapidly mobilised / water-soluble / used for short-term demand, whereas lipid is for long-term storage.
- **Further creditable point (A):** both are insoluble in stored form, so neither alters water potential — awarded if not already used.

**Why comparative phrasing matters.** The engine needs each point to link the TWO molecules. 'Lipids give 38 kJ g⁻¹' on its own is a fact, not a comparison; 'lipids give about twice the energy per gram of carbohydrate' is the comparative statement that scores. Two separate lists — one on carbohydrates, one on lipids — with no linking words will not be credited as comparisons even if every fact is correct.

**Accept equivalents and ECF.** Equivalent wording ('energy-dense', 'more reduced', 'stored without water') is accepted. The points are independent, so a wrong energy value in Point 2 does not stop Points 1, 3 and 4 scoring, provided each is a genuine comparison.

**Bottom line:** to secure all 4 marks, give at least one similarity and three contrasts, each written as a single sentence that names both carbohydrate and lipid. Distinct comparative points only — repeating the energy-density idea in two forms scores once.

sections.content: The Practice button marks YOUR answer this same way — point by point, crediting each distinct comparison, with ECF and a cap at the marks available. So write in comparative sentences: pair carbohydrate with lipid in every line ('lipids store more energy per gram than carbohydrates because…'), give one similarity and enough contrasts to cover the marks, and avoid two separate lists. Showing the comparison explicitly is what protects your score.
Where this leads

The ideas here run right through the course. Condensation and hydrolysis reappear for proteins and nucleic acids in later topics, so the water-in/water-out rule you learn on sugars is a template for every macromolecule. The phospholipid bilayer is the foundation of membrane structure and transport, and the energy in triglycerides feeds directly into cell respiration. Above all, keep the habit this topic teaches — read a molecule's structure and predict its function — because it is the single most powerful move in the whole of IB Biology.
